topic: Someone discovers their recipe has become famous under another name
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara traced the faded ink on the recipe card, the familiar scent of burnt sugar and vanilla suddenly feeling like a heavy cloak. It had been a quiet afternoon, just me and my grandmother’s old cookbook, when I found it. The recipe for her legendary lemon drizzle cake, the one that had graced countless local bake sales. Now, it was plastered across glossy magazines, attributed to 'The Golden Whisk.' My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming beneath my ribs. I reread the instructions, the precise measurements, the secret folding technique—all mine. Seeing that name, that glittering fame, attached to my humble creation felt like a physical blow. I picked up my phone, my fingers trembling slightly as I scrolled through social media, searching for any mention of 'The Golden Whisk,' a name I hadn't heard before. The silence in the kitchen amplified the frantic drumming in my chest.

[story 2]

"It's just... impossible," Mark muttered, staring blankly at the printout of the newspaper clipping. He hadn't touched the plate of cooling cookies in front of him. The sheer volume of articles praising the 'Azure Delight'—a name that sounded so sophisticated, so far removed from my own simple, handwritten notes—made his jaw tighten. He picked up the worn recipe book, his thumb brushing over the elegant script detailing the perfect ratio of zest to sugar. A wave of profound deflation washed over him, leaving him feeling hollowed out. He walked to the window, watching the bustling street below, feeling disconnected from the vibrant energy of the world. He wanted to shout, to demand that people recognize the true source, but the words caught in his throat, leaving only a dry, rasping sound.

[story 3]

I stood by the counter, the cooling rack holding the remnants of the batch I’d made yesterday. The glossy flyer for 'The Saffron Swirl' was taped crookedly to the fridge, mocking me with its vibrant, professional design. My hands started to shake, a fine tremor running through my fingers as I examined the text. It detailed the exact method for the spiced shortbread I’d perfected years ago. A strange, hot flush crept up my neck, making my cheeks feel tight and burning. I sank onto a stool, leaning my head back against the cool wood of the cabinet. Why couldn't they just remember *my* name? The realization settled in my gut like lead, heavy and suffocating. I closed my eyes, trying to steady my breathing, but the air felt thick, resistant to any easy release.
